---
title: "Automated observability with Puppet in a zero-trust environment"
format:
  revealjs:
    filters:
      - roughnotation
    theme: [dracula, custom.scss]
    slide-number: true
    logo: images/tkf-logo-title-white@2x-400x126.webp
    center: true
    title-slide-attributes:
      data-background-image: "images/convocation-hall.png"
      data-background-size: contain
    footer: '[TechKnowFile](https://techknowfile.utoronto.ca/)'
---

##

::: {.r-fit-text}
Haroon Rafique
:::

::: {style="font-size: 1.5em;"}
Manager  
Development and Operations  
Student Information Systems, ITS
:::

::: notes
Quick introduction and context for this session.
Set expectation: practical patterns, working code, and production-minded trade-offs.
:::

##

::: {style="font-size: 1.9em; font-weight: bold; line-height: 1.2; text-align: left;"}
_"If you want everything to be familiar, you will never learn anything new, because it can't be significantly different from what you already know."_
:::

::: {style="font-size: 0.9em; text-align: right; margin-top: 1em;"}
— Rich Hickey
:::
::: notes
I love this quote because it applies directly to how we build infrastructure.
We keep copy-pasting the same scripts and hand-maintaining files because it is
what we know. But that familiarity is actively holding us back from scaling.
To fix it, we have to recognize where we are getting stuck.
:::
---

## The Toil Trap

::: {.incremental .highlight-last}
- Manual monitoring target updates
- Config drift from reality 
- Onboarding friction due to cert. management
- YAML sprawl
:::

::: notes
Anchor on shared pain: most teams still run observability as a manual process.
New servers require manual monitoring target updates.
Certification management is a pain point.
Configuration is scattered across scripts, manifests, and YAML files, making
it hard to maintain and audit.
This is the bottleneck we want to remove.
:::

## Zero-Toil Architecture

::: {.incremental .highlight-last}
- Self-registration
- Automated discovery
- Identity-driven
- Zero-trust
:::

::: notes
To escape the toil trap, we must make observability and security native
properties of the system. And in this case self-registration and automated
discovery go hand in hand.
Let's look at the five patterns that make this target state possible.
:::

## Implementation Blueprint

::: {.incremental .highlight-last}
- Classify nodes automatically during CSR signing
- Separate logic from data using Hiera roles
- Automate service discovery with exported resources
- Secure metrics scraping with Caddy and mTLS
- Standardize monitoring with layered exporter profiles
:::

::: notes
This slide serves as our roadmap and contract for the rest of the presentation.
Every implementation pattern we cover next maps directly back to these five
foundational pillars
:::

## Pattern 1: CSR Classification

::: columns

::: {.column width="62%"}
::: {.incremental .highlight-last}
- Immutable identity requested during provisioning via CSR
- Cryptographic proof stored in the certificate extensions
- Tamper-proof classification secure against agent-side overrides
- Automated autosigning (optionally) enabled by secure policy scripts
:::
:::

::: {.column width="38%"}
::: {.fragment .fade-in-then-semi-out style="text-align: left;"}
What is `pp_role` and what goes into it?

- roles
  - monitoring_scraper
  - webserver

Hiera data:
```{.text code-line-numbers="false"}
roles
├── monitoring_scraper.yaml
└── webserver.yaml
```
:::
:::
:::

::: notes
Roles and profiles is not a feature provided by Puppet itself.
It is a best-practice design pattern for Puppet that provides an interface between
your business logic and reusable Puppet modules. At the moment of provisioning,
the node requests its specific role via the pp_role certificate extension.
This embeds its identity at birth and creates a tamper-proof source of truth that
agents cannot override. By keeping node intent attached directly to node identity,
we eliminate central merge conflicts in static files and build a pipeline
perfectly suited for dynamic, ephemeral fleets.
:::

## Pattern 2: Pure Data Roles

::: {.incremental .highlight-last}
- **Zero logic** inside site manifests or node definitions
  ```{.ruby filename="manifests/site.pp"}
  node default {
    # Lookup classes from Hiera based on pp_role
    # The pp_role comes from csr_attributes.yaml on the agent
    lookup('classes', Array[String], 'unique').include
  }
  ```

- CSR Extension attributes
  ```{.yaml filename="/etc/puppetlabs/puppet/csr_attributes.yaml"}
  ---
  extension_requests:
    pp_role: webserver
  ```

:::

::: notes
The node's role is purely data-driven and attached to the certificate.
The site manifest is just a thin layer that looks up classes based on the role.
:::

##

::: {.incremental .highlight-last}
- **Hiera lookup** determines config based on `pp_role`
  ```{.yaml filename="data/roles/webserver.yaml"}
  ---
  # Webserver role - Apache with monitoring
  # Applied to nodes with pp_role=webserver in csr_attributes.yaml
  classes:
  - profile::apache
  - profile::monitoring::apache_exporter
  ```

- Hiera configuration
  ```{.yaml filename="excerpt from hiera.yaml"}
  hierarchy:
  - name: "Per-role data (from CSR attributes)"
    path: "roles/%{trusted.extensions.pp_role}.yaml"
  ```
:::

## Pattern 3: Auto-Discovery
::: {.incremental .highlight-last}
- **Exported resources** publish state to OpenVoxDB
  ```ruby
  @@file { "/etc/vmagent/targets.d/${facts['networking']['fqdn']}_${name}.yaml":
    ensure  => file,
    content => @("EOT"),
      # Managed by Puppet
      - targets:
          - ${facts['networking']['fqdn']}:9090/${name}/metrics
        labels:
          job: ${name}
          instance: ${facts['networking']['fqdn']}
          exporter: ${name}
      | EOT
    tag     => 'vmagent_target',
  }
  ```
:::

::: notes
**This is the key automation mechanism of the entire talk. If you remember only
one thing from today, make it this pattern.**

Instead of manually updating static files, nodes now declare their own state at birth.
When a node compiles its catalog, it exports its scrape target and stores it into OpenVoxDB.
:::

##
::: {.incremental .highlight-last}
- **Dynamic collection** gathers all active targets
  ```ruby
  # Collect all exported vmagent targets from other nodes
  File <<| tag == 'vmagent_target' |>>
  ```
:::

::: notes
The server scraping the metrics, then queries OpenVoxDB, and dynamically pulls in
all active targets. This eliminates the need for human intervention
when scaling up new infrastructure.
:::

## Pattern 4: Zero-Trust mTLS
::: {.incremental .highlight-last}
- **Caddy** acts as a _sidecar_ reverse proxy and mTLS server
  ```{.text filename="excerpt from Caddyfile"}
  # Authenticate via Puppet CA with mTLS
  tls /etc/caddy/node-cert.pem /etc/caddy/node-key.pem {
    client_auth {
      mode require_and_verify
      trust_pool file /etc/caddy/puppet-ca.pem
    }
  }
  ```
- **Import** exporter configurations
  ```text
  import /etc/caddy/conf.d/*.caddy
  ```
:::
::: notes
To achieve zero-trust security by default, we cannot have metrics exposed over
plaintext HTTP. We use the existing Puppet CA to establish mutual TLS between
metrics scraper and our exporters.
:::

##

::: {.incremental .highlight-last}
- **Proxy** the exporter traffic
  ```{.text filename="excerpt from apache.caddy"}
  handle_path /apache* {
    reverse_proxy localhost:9117
  }
  ```
- Only allow **legitimate** scrapers
  ```caddyfile
  @authorized_scrapers_snippet {
    expression \
      {tls_client_subject} == "CN=vmagent.local"
  }
  ```
- **Block** unauthorized scrapers
  ```caddyfile
  handle {
   abort
  }
  ```
:::

::: notes
By placing Caddy as a lightweight sidecar, we secure the metrics path with zero
changes to the exporter code itself, ensuring only authenticated scrapers can
access the data.
:::

## Pattern 5: Layered Observability
::: {.incremental .highlight-last}
- **Common** profile applies to all nodes
  ```{.yaml filename="data/common.yaml"}
  classes:
  - profile::base
  ```
- **Baseline metrics** applied to every node
  ```{.ruby filename="site/profile/manifests/base.pp"}
  class profile::base {
    include profile::monitoring::node_exporter
  }
  ```
- **Role-specific** exporters added based on workload
  ```{.yaml filename="roles/webserver.yaml"}
  classes:
  - profile::apache
  - profile::monitoring::apache_exporter
    ```
:::
::: notes
To ensure comprehensive monitoring without creating alert fatigue, we take a
layered approach to our exporters. Every single node in the fleet automatically
receives a baseline OS-level node exporter via our base profile. Then, we use
our Hiera roles to dynamically layer on specialized exporters—like
PostgreSQL or NGINX—only on the nodes that are actually running those specific
workloads.
By keeping our baseline and role profiles perfectly aligned with our Hiera roles,
we enforce strict, predictable naming conventions and labels across the entire fleet.
we enforce strict, predictable naming conventions and labels across the entire fleet.
This is what finally allows us to use a single, unified query and dashboard model
across all of our environments, drastically reducing clutter in the visualization
layer.
:::


## Components in This Reference Stack

::: {.incremental .highlight-last}
- [OpenVox/OpenVox Server]{.rn-fragment rn-type=circle rn-color=yellow rn-strokeWidth=3 rn-padding=30} for policy and certificates
- [OpenVoxDB]{.rn-fragment rn-type=box rn-color=yellow rn-strokeWidth=3} for exported resource inventory
- [VictoriaMetrics]{.rn-fragment rn-type=highlight rn-color=purple} for metrics storage
- [Vmagent]{.rn-fragment rn-type=underline rn-color=red} for metrics scraping
- [Grafana]{.rn-fragment rn-type=highlight rn-color=tomato} for visualization
:::

::: notes
Keep this slide fast; details come later.
The goal is to orient the audience before implementation deep dive.
:::

## The Automated Pipeline

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
### Host Lifecycle {.fragment fragment-index=1}

::: {.fragment fragment-index=1}
- **Host boots** and starts the OpenVox agent.
:::
::: {.fragment fragment-index=3}
- **Agent submits** CSR with role intent.
:::
::: {.fragment fragment-index=5}
- **Host applies** role-specific profiles.
:::
::: {.fragment fragment-index=7}
- **Exporters publish** scrape endpoints.
:::
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
### System Response {.fragment fragment-index=2}

::: {.fragment fragment-index=2}
- **CA auto-signs** based on secure policy.
:::
::: {.fragment fragment-index=4}
- **Hiera maps** data classes to the host.
:::
::: {.fragment fragment-index=6}
- **OpenVoxDB stores** the exported assets.
:::
::: {.fragment fragment-index=8}
- **Vmagent polls** the target automatically.
:::
:::
:::

## Security Guardrails For Production

- **Strict validation** of role-bearing CSRs.
- **Role restrictions** to prevent privilege escalation.
- **Least-privilege paths** for authorized scrapers only.
- **Continuous auditing** of exported resources.

::: notes
We have to be explicit: complete automation must still be strictly governed.
Guardrails like policy-based autosigning scripts prevent rogue agents from
spoofing a privileged role or overexposing sensitive metric endpoints.
You can have absolute speed and zero-touch workflows without leaving your
infrastructure wide open.
:::

## Does It Work?

::: {.incremental .highlight-last}
- **Initial State:** Verify current targets are up.
- **The Trigger:** Provision a new webserver node.
- **The Magic:** Watch Puppet execute automatic classification.
- **The Result:** The new scrape target populates automatically.
- **The Payoff:** View the live Grafana dashboards.
:::

::: notes
To prove this isn't just theoretical vaporware, we are going to do a live run.
We will spin up a fresh node from scratch and watch it self-register, classify,
and populate directly into Grafana without us touching a single configuration file.
:::

## {.center}

::: {.r-fit-text}
Demo
:::

## Operational Impact

- **Scales infinitely** from isolated labs to massive production fleets.
- **Adapts dynamically** to volatile node churn and auto-scaling.
- **Accelerates visibility** by establishing instant scrape targets.
- **Maintains effort** keeping operational overhead flat as nodes scale.

::: notes
To tie this back to the operational economics: this architecture completely breaks
the linear relationship between the size of your fleet and the size of your
monitoring workload. By removing manual interventions, we ensure that adding
1,000 new nodes requires the exact same operator effort as adding a single host,
while actively lowering the rate of outages caused by human configuration drift.
:::

## Engineering Anti-Patterns

- **Mutable scripts** installing runtime packages
- **Floating tags** using `latest` in production
- **Hard-coded credentials** in compose files
- **Copy-pasted logic** repeated across services
- **Ungoverned attributes** trusted without policy controls

::: notes
To wrap things up, these are the real-world traps I ran into while building out
this live demo environment. Treating this slide as a transition into future
hardening work will ensure your automation doesn't accidentally introduce
security blind spots or fragile dependencies.
:::

## Key Takeaways

- **Automate observability** from the exact second a node boot
- **Eliminate bottlenecks** by moving classification to CSRs.
- **Drop manual** updates using native exported resources
- **Recycle existing security** to achieve zero-trust mTLS
- **Scale your infrastructure** without increasing team toil

::: notes
To wrap up today's session, these are the core architectural pillars I hope you
take back to your teams. I invite you all to follow up with me to look at the
repository, review the Caddy and Puppet configurations, and discuss how to
apply these execution patterns to your own environments.
:::

## {.center}

::: {.r-fit-text}
Questions?
:::

## Git Repo

![](images/zero-trust-observability.svg){width="30%"}
